courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: University and Society
courses.description: This course aims to provide an opportunity for students to know more about the history of university, its social roles, the relation between tertiary education and development of individual/society.
courses.enrollment_requirement: This course is for Shaw College students and first year students only.
courses.learning_outcomes:
University and Society -learning outcomes

1. To recognize key concepts, theories and issues regarding the roles of university education.

2. To identify and articulate their goals in university learning and campus life, and to develop strategies to achieve them

3. To discuss and evaluate the visions and missions of The Chinese University of Hong Kong and the Shaw College and to reflect their own role in the wider community.

4. To apply skills in information gathering, processing and analysis in the enhancement of their studying.
courses.course_syllabus:
GESC 1130 Syllabus

* *第一部分︰你在大學，學些甚麼？**

第一講︰課程簡介、關鍵字介紹：何謂大學／何謂做論文／何謂研究／知識產權與大學教育的關係等

第二講：在大學的教與學：讀書方法／學問的累積／建立一套個人的思想

第三講：大學在社會中的影響︰大學與社會發展的關係／研究、論述與政府及其他社會組織的關係及影響

* *第二部分：大學在社會**

第四講：東西方各家看知識分子：知識分子是技術人員？／社會良心？／純學知識的創造者？

第五講：關於現代大學的一些爭論議題：學術自由的論爭／政府與大學的關係／大學應培養通材還是專業人士等

第六講：大學教育在香港：大學教育在港歷史、大學教育在港的特色與角色、政府及各種撥款委員會與大學的關係，等

第七講：認識香港中文大學：從香港社會發展的歷程，理解中文大學的特色與角色

* *第三部分︰大學與大學生**

第八講︰如何規劃你的大學生活（一）︰從認識自己、認識社會／尋找個人生活的方向與目標

第九講︰如何規劃你的大學生活（二）如何規劃未來的生活／一些大學生活規劃的建議

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Environmental Problems in Hong Kong
courses.description: The purposes of this course are to: (1) introduce students to the environmental problems encountered in Hong Kong; (2) help students to understand the scientific nature of these environmental problems; (3) enable students to use scientific concepts developed in the course to evaluate the options available in dealing with these environmental problems; and (4) provide students with a general overview of the Hong Kong Government's environmental programmes. (This course is suitable for first year students.)
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College students only. Not for Biology, Environmental Science and Geography and Resource Management Majors.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Recognize the major environmental problems in Hong Kong.
2. Understand the scientific basis of air, noise and water pollution.
3. Understand the problem of waste disposal and waste management in Hong Kong.
4. Understand the role played by Hong Kong people in the global biodiversity crisis.
5. Apply scientific knowledge to: i) examine all aspects of environmental issues, ii) make scientific judgments about environmental issues, and iii) critically evaluate the Hong Kong Government’s environmental programmes, strategies and policies.
6. Apply scientific and ecological knowledge to live in an environmentally friendly and sustainable manner.
courses.course_syllabus:
Major concepts include:
1) Sustainable development
2) Urban planning policy
3) Air pollution
4) Noise pollution
5) Solid waste management
6) Water pollution
7) Conservation of biodiversity
8) Environmental ethics and economics

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Science and Our Lives
courses.description: This General Education course is jointly offered by the Biology Department and Shaw College. This course introduces students to biological issues closely related to our daily life. More specifically, issues with different levels of complexity, ranging from cellular to ecosystem dynamics that form the intimate and integrated part of our very own existence will be discussed. Topics related to medical, genetic, physiological, ecological and environmental issues will be covered. Specific contents of the course may change according to timely issues. Students will gain a general appreciation of how our lives are closely linked with changes and developments in the biological systems around us.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College students. Not for students of Fac of Engineering; Fac of Medicine or Fac of Science. Not for students of Interdisciplinary Data Analytics & X; Aerospace Science and Earth Informatics & X; Materials Science and Engineering & X.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To obtain a basic understanding of how the biological system works around us.

2. To understand how changes and developments in the biological system are closely linked to our daily life.

3. To appreciate latest developments in research on our biological system.

4. With the knowledge gained, to become more responsive citizens to issues related to changes and developments in the biological system around us.
courses.course_syllabus:
Major topics to be covered include, but not limited to the followings:

1. Emergence of life and evolution

2. Cellular mechanisms and processes

3. Animal and plant behaviour

4. Physiological dynamics and adaptations

5. Population and community interactions

6. Ecosystem functions and services

7. Biological application and technology

Specific topics will be taken up depending on timely issues, to make the course more relevant to our daily life.

courses.description: Aligning the founding aspiration of the College, “Learning, innovation, and benefitting humankind”, this course provides students with opportunities to apply their academic skills and knowledge to care for and serve the people in the society. It also enhances the understanding of students of community issues, as well as the capacities and assets of the community, with an aim to promote students’ apprehension of social inclusion. Students will engage in experiential learning to critically study and apply concepts and theories in an authentic, real-world environment through social servicing. To prepare students to care for others, they will first learn to care for themselves through developing loving-kindness, empathy, mindfulness, and respect for diversity. Students will review and reflect on the strengths and real-life needs of the community. Guided by course instructors, students will design and implement service-learning projects by themselves or in association with non-government organizations, social enterprises, or volunteer groups as host organizations. The course concludes with a post-service review in which students will share and reflect on their service-learning experience, re-examine their own self to gain deeper and broader appreciation of how their own discipline and academic knowledge relates to the society, and develop an enhanced sense of civic responsibility and a lifelong commitment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College students only. Not for students who have taken GESC2330, 2360 or ELTU2008.

2. Appreciating Service learning

a. Values and attitudes of service learning

b. Process and outcomes of service learning

3. Community and Societal Issues

a. Social policy and welfare

b. Poverty, elderly, ageing, social inclusion

4. Community Work and Service Techniques

a. Needs assessment

b. Asset-based community development

c. Community engagement

5. Program Development and Evaluation

a. Social innovation

b. Social impact assessment

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Buddhism As Lived: Mind, Body and Contemporary Issues
courses.description:
Many of us have been brought up thinking that Buddhism, like many religions, is a body of doctrines and beliefs laid down by religious authorities. Such an understanding, however, would render it lifeless and irrelevant to the everyday life of the believers. In fact, the lived experiences of the believers also contribute to the particularities of Buddhist traditions established worldwide.

This course aims at exploring the traditional and contemporary ways by which Buddhist teachings are expressed and experienced in the lives of the believers, in groups and individually. There are three major components to this course. First, we shall discuss the body-mind relation, and the nurturing of mental and physical health with topics such as dietary practices, meditation, mindfulness, counselling, and Buddhist psychology. We shall also contemplate on the cultural manifestations of Buddhism in literature, popular culture, language, photography, film, folk custom, etc. Finally, we shall consider the light that Buddhism may shed on topics of contemporary life such as love, death, transgender issues.

No prior knowledge of Buddhism is necessary.

SDG-SE #3 Good Health and Well-being:
This course aims at relating students’ personal growth and everyday life to Buddhist teachings so as to help them cultivate a constructive outlook on self-identity, and understand the Buddhist ways in promoting healthy lives and well-being.

| 12 | Buddhist views on Death | *Samsara*, Buddhist Thanatology |

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Startup Exploration
courses.description: The objective of this course is to encourage students to explore the start-up journey and gain entrepreneurship experience. Through project-based learning, students will try to form a team to co-work together to define a start-up project that they want to do in this course. With this ownership of the project, they will also learn how to collaborate, communicate, contribute to the project. With the tailormade lectures based on the roadmap of start-up, including the session about impact to society (introduction to United Nation’s 17 SDGs), students will learn different start-up skillsets step by step. In addition, each group of the start-up team will be assigned an industrial mentor to share experience and guidance to the team to upgrade the business plan to a realistic plan. Finally, students will have a presentation in the demo day to share their projects to potential investors. After this course, students are invited to join CUHK Entrepreneurs Day organized by CUHK Alumni Affairs Office. Thus, this course provides whole-person training to encourage students to be innovative and have entrepreneurship spirit.

courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:

Knowledge and Cognitive Outcomes:

* acquire some of the basic concepts of setting up a company with consideration of the 17 SDGs
* learn to write a business plan
* comprehend making a plan and executing a project
* comprehend preparing a deck for presentation for introducing business ideas
* identify kinds of available funding and how to apply
* understand how to base on market demand in different applications with alignment of SDGs to create startup ideas and select an idea for startup by doing market research, data analysis and evaluation

Attitude Outcomes:

* develop a positive attitude to form team and collaborate with different people
* appreciate to respect and co-work with other teammates from different background
* build up a positive attitude and sense of responsibility towards the fulfilment of SDGs
* learn and adapt to work under pressure
* become prepared to present to a target audience of business pitch (e.g. potential investors)
* gain experience from making mistakes and fine tuning to right direction

Skill Outcomes:

* develop creativity and generate ideas
* understand the impact investment requirement based on the learning from SDGs and align with the project objective of the startup
* gain experience to convince people through an elevator pitch presentation
* gain experience in public speech

In relation to SDGs:

* identify the major problems that the SDGs addresses
* analyse those problems from the relevant discipline perspectives
* investigate potential solutions for solving those problems
* evaluate the barriers to the implementation of those solutions; and
* reflect on the role they can play to meet the challenges through their proposed projects
courses.course_syllabus:
Core Content and Weekly Topics:

1. Introduction to Company and VMV (Vision, Mission and Value)

In lecture 1, the trends of economy, 17 SDGs, startup, innovation and technology, social innovation etc. will be covered. Market need exploration exercise will be conducted in class. Based on this, students will then learn how to set the company vision, mission and value.

2. Understanding the Team and Team Formation

In lecture 2, the importance of team formation and the method to form team will be covered. Based on the students’ submission of their company VMV and target market need done in the previous lecture, students with similar VMV will be grouped. Round table discussion session will be arranged to facilitate the final team formation with the biography of each member and finalise the company VMV. Students will discuss and learn more about what SDGs they are going to achieve and what other partners they can collaborate to achieve SDG17.

3. Product and Service

In lecture 3, the method of identifying the product and service (including feature lists, specification and product and service introduction and description) based on the market need and company VMV will be covered. Team discussion and in class exercise for product and service will be arranged. Students will learn how their proposed solutions to achieve different SDGs and fulfil market needs.

4. Development Roadmap

In lecture 4, the project management for defining the solution development roadmap based on proposed product and service will be covered. Team discussion and in class exercise for the development plan and roadmap will be arranged. Students will learn how to set up milestones for their solution development in the development plan.

5. Technology Trend

In lecture 5, the introduction of different technologies and trends will be covered. Team discussion and in class exercise for selecting suitable technology to enhance their solutions will be arranged. Students will learn how to adopt different technologies in their solutions and refine their development plan.

6. Introduction to Impact (United Nation’s 17 SDGs) and ESG (Environmental, Social and Governance)

In lecture 6, the introduction of impact investment (about how to measure the impact based on SDGs) and fundraising will be covered. Examples on different projects for different SDGs will be shown. Team discussion and in class exercise for impact plan for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to make use of their solutions to fulfil different SDGs and develop their impact plan.

7. Business Model

In lecture 7, the introduction of business models will be covered. Examples on different projects for different business models will be shown. Team discussion and in class exercise for business models for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to select a suitable business model or even create a new business model for their solutions.

8. Sales Marketing

In lecture 8, the introduction of sales and marketing will be covered. Examples about different sales and marketing strategies will be shown. Team discussion and in class exercise for sales and market strategies for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to select a suitable sales and marketing strategy or even create a new sales and marketing strategy for their solutions.

9. Account and Finance

In lecture 9, the introduction of account and finance will be covered. Examples about different assumptions to estimate the forecast of the sales will be shown. Team discussion and in class exercise for financial plan and forecast projection for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to make a financial plan as well as forecast projection.

10. Budget and Funding

In lecture 10, the introduction of budgeting for setting up a startup and available funding will be covered. Team discussion and in class exercise for budget plan and startup plan for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to make a startup plan with budget.

11. Investment Deck

In lecture 11, the introduction of different types of investment deck will be covered. Examples about different types of investment decks will be shown. Team discussion and in class exercise for preparing the investment decks for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to make an investment deck.

12. Evaluator Pitch

In lecture 12, the introduction of the skills for evaluator pitch will be covered. Examples about successful evaluator pitch will be shown. Team discussion and in class exercise for the preparation of evaluator pitch and how to do story telling for their projects will be arranged. Students will learn how to do evaluator pitch.

13. Demo Day

In lecture 13, All the students will conduct a group presentation by integration of all work done in previous lectures. Students will experience how to do pitching and learn from other teams.

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: The Hong Kong Story
courses.description: Tracing the transition from British colonial rule to the establishment of the HKSAR, this course explores the many transformations and milestones that have shaped the Hong Kong story. Designed from a sociological perspective, but specifically for students without sociological background or much background knowledge about Hong Kong, this course shall guide them through the journey of Hong Kong and its people so far. It will pick up popular images about Hong Kong, such as "a laissez-faire economic miracle", "the safest place in the world", or "the land of opportunity", evaluating their validities or tracing their origins and implications. Selected contemporary issues arising from the city’s sustainable development will also be discussed, such as migration, inequality, and affordable housing. Integrating experiential learning and e-learning strategies, this course should offer students a range of new learning experiences and new angles of approaching the Hong Kong society.

courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students should be able to:

1. Go beyond the constraint of individual experiences and recognize the broader influence of Hong Kong’s post-war social developments on Hong Kong people's personal biography and community living.
2. Identify and critically discuss dominant imageries of social life in Hong Kong.
3. Analyze everyday encounters in the community of Hong Kong and evaluate their broader significance with consolidated knowledge and perspectives gained from this course.
4. Evaluate and reflect on selected issues concerning the sustainable development of Hong Kong
5. Engage in self-initiated learning processes with technological aid geared towards active discovery.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. ​Introduction – The Hong Kong spectacle

2. Hong Kong as a crown jewel of the British Empire: Colonial history and legacy

3. Hong Kong as an international hub: The rise of Hong Kong in the world-system

4. Hong Kong as an economic miracle? The myth of laissez-faire and industrialization

5. Hong Kong as a migration center: Cultural diversity and social polarization in Hong Kong
– SDG#1, 8, 10 – this lecture will discuss migration in HK with reference to SDG indicators that are ‘migration-related’ and ‘disaggregation’ related . Specifically, the lecture will discuss protection of migrant labour rights and promote safe and secure migrant working environment (8.8); facilitation of orderly, safe, regular and responsible migration (10.7), reducing poverty and better social protection of all population members, such as ethnic minorities in HK (1.3.1).

6. Hong Kong as a place of conflict over hearts and minds: identity politics beyond left and right

7. Hong Kong as a divided society: From land of opportunity to land of inequality?
– SDG#10 – this lecture will introduce the concept of inequality and examine the evolving social mobility system of HK and the inequalities in outcome (10.3), hence giving students insights on methods to reduce HK’s inequality (e.g. wage and social protection policies 10.4).

8. Hong Kong as an urban jungle: Affordable housing in Hong Kong
– SDG#11 – this lecture will examine HK’s current housing problem within the SDG framework of adequate, safe and affordable housing (11.1). Comparison will be made with overseas cases to discuss how to enhance inclusive and sustainable urbanization in the case of HK (11.3).

9. Hong Kong as the safest place in the world? From gang-land to cop-land to Disneyland

10. Hong Kong as a city of protest: Social movements past and now

11. Hollywood of the East: Cultural industry in Hong Kong

12. Conclusion: Cosmopolitan Hong Kong

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Film Genres: Love, Death and Laughs
courses.description:
(GESC2010 is double-coded with COMM2932.)
This course focuses on a variety of film genres, such as detective and gangster film, musicals, comedies, family dramas and westerns. Genres have been popular since the beginning of the film medium and remain quite popular today. This class will examine the cultural and economic reasons for their continuing popularity. Film showings and film clips will demonstrate how genres evolve over time and how some of the great film directors have made genre films of lasting impact. This class assumes no prior knowledge of film. Students will be assessed on the basis of a paper and an examination.

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Debating Globalization
courses.description: Through a combination of experiential learning activities and web-based delivery, this course aims at expose students to a number of intense debates surrounding globalization such as free trade, global culture, national sovereignty, the question of multinational corporations and labour rights and the anti-globalization movement. The objective is to build basic knowledge of the opposing views on globalization, their political and ideological bases, and implications for political and personal actions. Specifically, the course will analyse and evaluate selected globalization debates under the framework of United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Through the experiential learning activities built in the course, we aim at helping students develop the sensitivity and competences necessary for global readiness and to articulate their personal positions on a sustainable future.

1. To recognize the key concepts, theories and issues surrounding the debates of globalization.
2. To interpret and discuss the relevance to and impacts of globalization on everyday life experiences.
3. To analyse and evaluate selected globalization debates under the framework of UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)
4. To demonstrate personal learning experience through activities in the context of globalization in daily life.

courses.course_syllabus:
1. Global Issues, Big Debates: History and Perspectives on globalization.

2. Global Production or New Sweatshops? (SDG 8, 10)

– this lecture discusses how the economic gain (8) under current global division of labor can help to reduce inequality (10), and will visit various labor and environmental issues arising from the flexible production process (8).

3. Free trade, WTO and the Food Regime. (SDG 2, 3)

– this lecture discusses the impact of liberalizing trade on global food regime (2) and examine if the liberalized food market is first: providing healthier or diversified food source (3) and second: providing equal access to food or creating more food waste.

4. Financial Globalization: Crises and Innovations (SDG 1, 10)

– this lecture examines the impact of liberalization of finance on poverty and financialization on people’s life, with a comparative angle between developed and developing world (1, 10), for example discussing the role of mobile financing and micro-loan in tackling general poverty and reducing gender inequality.

5. The Nation-State under Threats?

6. Globalization for Peace: World without Strangers or Clash of Civilizations? (SDG 16)

– this lecture examines basic tenet of international peace mechanism and how identity politics arising from globalization impact on global institutions and related consequences (16)

7. Migration in a Borderless World? (SDG 10, 11, 16)

– this lecture discusses the turbulence of migration under globalization and how it could help reduce inequality in developing world through remittances (10). It will discuss the current situation migrant labors and refugees and how enhancing their status through strengthened institutions could lead to more sustainable city development (11, 16)

8. Cultural Globalization: Creative acculturation or homogenization?

9. Globalization and Environment (SDGs 11, 13, 14)

– this lecture discusses climate change (13) and sustainable development in global fishery industry (14) with a comparative angle between developed and developing countries, cities and coastal communities (11)

10. Globalization and Inequality (SDGs 1, 2, 10)

– this lecture focuses on whether and how globalization impact on inequality between and within countries (1, 10). Selected international programmes that aim at reducing poverty and hunger, and the mechanism behind, will also be discussed (2)

11. From Rise of Global Civil Society to Privatization of Regulation: Development in Global Justice Movement. (SDG 17)

– with reference made to UN Global Compact, a particular focus of this lecture is on partnership between various stakeholders in enhancing a a better globalization (17).

12. Making sense of globalization: from Everyday Life to Global Readiness.

– this lecture concludes by inviting students to reflect on their everyday lifestyle (e.g. consumption, life-chance) and ways to live or strive for a more sustainable future.

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Questions of a Modern Person
courses.description: By introducing students to the socio-economic, political and cultural foundation of complex and pluralistic modern societies, this course will help first year university students reflect on the key issues they will encounter in their transition to adulthood and adjustment to this new phase of their personal development. This course attempts to provide students with a wider interdisciplinary perspective, to introduce them to different relevant factors and various ways of approach to the problems of modern person. We hope to enhance their self-awareness of the need of recognizing differences and being tolerant, as well as the diversity of moral values and beliefs in modern society. There will be lectures, discussions, debates, multi-media presentations, interviews, on-site visits wherever and whenever appropriate and available.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Questions of a Modern Person - learning outcomes

1. To recognize key concepts, theories and issues regarding the formation of modernity and the mentality of modern person.

2. To identify the major social forces that shape students’ transition to adulthood and their impacts on their adjustment to university life.

3. To recognize the diversity of values and beliefs in modern societies as well as the origins of such diversity.

4. To apply the theoretical knowledge in the reflection of their role as university student in society and to relate this reflection to their status of future community leaders.

5. To demonstrate through project and other learning activities the skills in information gathering and processing, as well as the ability to cooperate with other people in team work.
courses.course_syllabus:
GESC2111 Syllabus

* *第一部分：導論──甚麼是「現代」？**

第一講：課程簡介、要求

第二講：現代以前──「傳統社會」中的人與社會／傳統社會是怎樣的？／人與土地的結連／人與宗教／各種人際間的紐帶

第三講：現代之產生──塑造現代社會的幾種主要趨勢／啓蒙運動／法國革命／工業革命／俄國革命等

* *第二部分：影響現今世的幾種力量**

第四講：全球代的年代／資訊科技的影響／新自由主義經濟的影響及其反省

第五講：文明衝突論？／介紹亨廷頓「文明衝突論」及有關辯論／一種多元文化的世界格局對我們的意義

* *第三部分：在現代社會的「自我」（self****）**

第六講：無處不在的「異化」（alienation）：甚麼是「異化」？／自由市場制度如何看「人」／科層組織（bureaucracy）下的「自我」

第七講：我消費，故我在（I consume, therefore I am）／消費主義的社會政治基礎

第八講：自戀文化（Narcissistic culture）與輔導世紀（theraptical age）：將所注意集中到自己身上／從減肥纖體、自我提升（self enhancement）到心靈輔導

第九講：現代社會的男、女、兒意與家庭：在現代社會之下的男、女性角色／家庭之轉變／現代社會下的家庭、兒童、青年

第十講：風險社會／現代文明的「回力標」／「個人化」風險與青少年成長

第十一講：後現代的問題：甚麼是後現代？／在後現代的狀態下，如何看個人與其他人的關係／如何看個人生活的目標

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Challenges of University Life
courses.description: University students are in transition from late adolescence to early adulthood. They face increasingly demanding life challenges. This course covers various issues related to psychosocial adjustment and personal development in early adulthood. Throughout this course, students will learn about the challenges of adult life, factors affecting physical and mental health, emotional awareness and emotion regulation, stress and stress management, development of self-identity, interpersonal relationship and communication, sexuality, and career development.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College students only. Students who have taken UGED1600 or SOWK1150 cannot take this course to fulfil General Education requirements.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *Learning Goals**
1. To inform students about life challenges that they will face in early adulthood.
2. To help students develop a healthy life style (e.g., eat well, sleep well and exercise well).
3. To enhance students’ emotional awareness and emotion regulation ability.
4. To help students develop better self-awareness.
5. To enhance students’ interpersonal skills.
6. To help students plan for their future career development.

* *Learning Outcomes**

1. Get familiar and prepared to face various life challenges in early adulthood.
2. Enhance resilience under stress in early adulthood.
3. Personal growth, both emotionally and cognitively.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. From adolescence to adulthood: Going through changes
2. Psychology and the challenges of adult life
3. Factors affecting physical and mental health
4. Developing a healthy life style
5. Emotional awareness and regulation
6. Stress and stress management
7. The self in a social world
8. Social influence: Being influenced by and influencing others
9. Interpersonal attraction: Of friendship and love
10. Relationships and communication
11. Gender and Sexuality
12. The challenge of the workplace

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Comprehending the City
courses.description: City living is a modern way of life and sustainability has become the central issue of urban development. By 2020, it is expected that about 60% of the worlds’s population will live in cities. Cities are thus the places where touch most the quality of life. With the development of economy, society and technology, cities have undergone dramatic changes in their strength, structure and direction. Some cities continue to prosper while others experience declining. How individuals and urban communities can survive and even prosper demands a good understanding of the urban systems. This course will be devoted to the investigation of the growth dynamics of cities, their economic, social and environmental problems and the ways to develop sustainable cities. Many daily life examples of Hong Kong and other cities in China and western countries will be used to illustrate various urban contradictions and problems. After being equipped with the knowledge of various urban issues and problems, students are expected to contribute their knowledge to the urban communities by participating well planned service learning projects in this course and then further enhance their ability to learn effectively through service. After taking this course, students will be well-prepared for challenges in world cities such as Hong Kong and equipped with the knowledge and the vision to become a responsible and concerned urban resident with a passion of caring for people and communities.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College Students only. Not for Geography and Resource Management Majors.
courses.learning_outcomes:
GESC2131\_outcomes

Cities are important places for living, studying, working and contributing to the society. However, cities are complicated with various attractions and problems. After taking this course, students will have:

1. Demonstrated a general and multi-faceted understanding of the diversity and complexity of modern cities;

2. Assessed the contradictive natures of the cities;

3. Criticizied the various urban problems in Hong Kong; and

4. Developed the ability to contribute urban knowledge to the communities.

Along with the knowledge enhancement on cities, students will be able to further improve their critical thinking and analytical capability through problem-based teaching and service learning approach.
courses.course_syllabus:
GESC2131 Syllabus

Course Syllabus:

1. City: an important stage of civilization

2. The process of urbanization

3. The engine of urban growth: economic base of a city

4. Urban land use and regulation

5. Housing the people: the market and the government

6. Moving the people: transport and mobility

7. Urban mosaic and spatial segregation

8. Affluence and poverty in the city

9. Population problems in the city

10. Urban decay and renewal

11. Globalization and world cities

12. Urban environmental problems and sustainability

13. Is a city sustainable?

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Reconstruction of Hong Kong History and Social Development
courses.description: This course introduces basic research methodologies on Hong Kong history, which include the use of primary sources, oral history materials and analysis of secondary sources. Through the evaluation of the characteristics of different interpretations on major Hong Kong historical events and personalities since 1840 and its impacts on the development of Hong Kong society, students can re-examine their local cultural heritage and their commitment to the community. Field trips and topical studies will be organized to explore the evolution of local society through personal experience.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College students only. Not for students who have taken UGEC2935 or 2945.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Provide background knowledge on Hong Kong history to prepare students for their future careers.

2. Broaden students learning experiences during their undergraduate studies.

3. Deepen their understanding of local heritage cultures through experiential learning.

4. Learn basic research skills of conducting research on local history and enhance their writing skills.

5. Enhance inter-personal skills with interviewees through oral history exercises.

6. Develop critical thinking through analysis on historical materials.

7. Build up learning interest as a lifelong process.

- 認識香港史原始資料 Understanding of Primary Sources

- 香港歷史人物 Historical Events in Hong Kong

- 近代香港社會結構的特徵 Characteristics of Social Structure in Modern Hong Kong Society

- 香港本土意識的誕生與傳承 Birth of Local Identity and History Heritage

- 社會發展的局限與潛力 Limits and Potential of Social Development

- 資料蒐集與處理 Collection and Classification of Historical Materials

- 實地考察 Field Studies

- 撰寫香港史研究報告 Writing of Hong Kong History Reports

- 香港社會發展與你 Hong Kong Social Development and You

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: From Page to Stage-Exploring the Art of Drama and Theatre
courses.description:
Through lecture and seminar, students will be introduced to the following famous plays in the world: A Doll’s House, Death of a Salesman, Waiting For Godot, Thunder Storm, and The Silly Girl and the Funny Old Tree. The latter part of the course will be dedicated to practical work including rehearsals and technical theatre operations in attempts to stage excerpts from these plays. Students will be divided into groups of 5. As such, the plays covered in the course will be introducing to the students a good spectrum of world dramatic history and literature. The Shaw College Lecture Theatre will be used as the teaching, rehearsing and performing venue.

Offerings with different topics can be taken for credit up to a maximum of two times.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will acquire basic understanding of world drama throughout the ages including play analysis and critique.

2. Students will discover the differentiation between serious art, which is directly related to living and humanity, and entertainment.

3. Students will operate in a complete cycle of theatre production.

4. What would result is that personal speech and communication skills will be enhanced.

5. Students will learn to employ what they have learned in an environment of teamwork and cooperation in the theatrical production in their later lives.

6. Students will be able to identify and judge the results of the knowledge of the arts in general.

7. Personal abilities such as self-esteem and attributes such as latent talents will be enhanced.

8. Some students will demonstrate their potentials as leaders as the course is also about leadership training.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. The forms of drama, viz. tragedy, comedy, tragi-comedy, farce and melodrama.

2. The six elements of drama according to Aristotle.

3. The linear structure in conventional drama.

4. Styles of theatre from Classicism to Post-modernism.

5. The dramaturgy of the 5 plays mentioned above.

6. The theatre production procedures – from rehearsal to opening.

7. Lighting, scenorgraphy and sound design

8. Stage management

9. The working schedules

10. The work of a theatre producer – marketing, publicity, ticketing and customer service.

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Service Learning: Human Concern
courses.description: This is a Service-Learning course which integrates meaningful social service activity with instruction and critical reflection in class to enrich the learning and the betterment of society, aims to foster students’ humanism through civic participation. Based on the principles of Service-Learning, volunteerism and reflective learning, students are required to apply theories and concepts to design and implement a series of services for a specialized community. This course corresponds to SDG#10 - reducing inequality, promoting an inclusive society of all, Irrespective of age, gender, disability, ethnicity, or socio-economic status. Throughout group discussion, self-reflections, presentations and onsite supervision of community service projects, including direct service, inquiry and advocacy experiences, students will be facilitated to develop in-depth self-reflection and understanding on the needs of the socially disadvantaged groups, as well as the relationship between individuals and the society. It is expected that students are able to develop a long-term civic commitment to the well-being and betterment of society.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For Shaw College students only. Not for Social Work Major, and students who have taken GESC1230, 2360 or ELTU2008.
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-SE #10 Reducing Inequality
Experiential Learning Course
Service Learning Course
courses.course_syllabus:
The major concepts and topics to be covered in this course are:

1. Principles and values of service learning

- Roots and perspectives of service learning

- Individuals and the society: altruism and volunteerism

- Civic responsibility and commitment to the betterment of community

2. Design Thinking

- Knowledge and skills in identifying problems and empathizing needs of community

- Need assessment strategy and techniques through community observation , statistics and interview

- Define the objectives of the project

- Ideate and brainstorm innovative service ideas

3. Service techniques

- Project planning and implementation

- Program evaluation and social impact assessment

4. Principles of experiential and reflective learning

- Contemplate the meaning of the service

- Evaluate its context and impact

- Reach a greater understanding of the self, the learning and the society

Dr. NGAI Hung Kui

Dr. LO Fai Hang,

courses.description: This course intends to introduce basic concepts of gene functions, genetic engineering, synthetic biology and the impacts of gene technology on our society. Since the genetic sequences of human beings and many different organisms become available, their role in biological processes and diseases become more understood and comprehensible to human beings. Possibilities of making good use of such bioinformatics knowledge and manipulation of genes have opened a brave new world for us. Genetic engineering of biological organisms aims to help human beings resolve problems associated with our sustainability on planet earth in various aspects of health and medical cares, food and energy supply, environmental sustainability, industrial production and monitoring, etc, all of which have significant impacts on our society as a whole, despite some might cause much debate.

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Appreciating Cantonese Opera
courses.description: This course gives an introduction to students on how to appreciate Cantonese opera. Learning to appreciate and understand the Cantonese opera and the relationship with its contexts may enhance students’ understanding of the Chinese culture and aesthetics, as well as improve students’ personal morality. Both lectures and field visits will be arranged to describe the workplace requirements and demonstrate positive value, professional attitude and work ethics through Cantonese opera activities. Students will be taught the aesthetics and practices of this art form and encouraged to participate in performance, creation and research of Cantonese opera.

courses.subject: GESC
courses.title: Emotion Regulation and Mental Health
courses.description: Emotion represents one of the most important forces that energizes and directs our behavior. Emotion dysregulation, however, disrupts normal mental processes and impairs psychosocial functioning. This course is designed to provide students with the most updated scientific knowledge about what emotions are, how emotion dysregulation results in different emotional disorders (e.g. anxiety disorders and mood disorders), and what one can do to overcome emotional disturbances and transform emotions into positive forces which serve adaptive purposes. Throughout this course, students will learn about the nature of emotions, how emotions affect different mental processes, symptoms of different emotional disorders, and as a preventive measure, different effective techniques of emotion regulation.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. For Shaw College students only.
2. Not for Psychology Majors.
3. Not for students who have taken UGED1571.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students will have:

a. learned about the nature of human emotions and how emotions affect different mental processes;

b. learned about how emotion dysregulation results in various common emotional disturbances; and

c. developed effective emotion regulation techniques and apply them in daily life.

2. Emotion dysregulation and emotional disorders

3. Anxiety dysregulation and anxiety disorders

4. Anger dysregulation and aggressive disorders

5. Mood dysregulation and mood disorders

6. Emotion dysregulation and somatoform disorders

7. Emotion dysregulation and sexual disorders

8. Emotion dysregulation and substance-use disorders

9. Impulse dysregulation and impulse-control disorders

10. Emotion regulation training I: Relaxation techniques

11. Emotion regulation training II: Breathing techniques

12. Emotion regulation training III: Imagery techniques

13. Emotion regulation training IV: Mindfulness techniques
